comment.body: Thank you for Alma's post, which also answered my previous question. The chaos of humanity is largely driven by self-interest, and since humans are not machines, irrational emotions often push the world to extremes. That’s why I’ve said before—despite his political goals—Trump’s political rhetoric and tactics further amplify humanity’s irrational emotions, bringing unnecessary trouble to the U.S. or even the entire world.

comment.body: For China, the development of semiconductor technology is inevitable. Taiwan is a territorial issue for China—an internal matter, not a problem related to TSMC. What China truly finds intolerable is the U.S. long-term strategy of using Taiwan to contain China, whether through arms sales or nurturing pro-American political factions.

comment.body: Evil Ferguson quote flys in the face of history. In the 1820’s Great Britain had a huge budget deficit of 200-250% of GDP because of the Napoleonic wars. It’s military spending was £15-20 mill and it’s interest expense was £28-30 mill. UK GDP was approx £500 mill. Yet the British empire grew thru the whole of the 19th century. I think the caveat on that on that quote probably needs to be about Gov total spending, that maybe more important. UK gov total spending in 1820’s was ££50-60 mill. So it paid the debt, maintain the military and maintained the empire for 10-12% of GDP.

comment.body: I’m not sure you can separate the territorial issues completely from the technological. Sure China could invade Taiwan at the drop of a hat but currently they could not hold it. That won’t change until their ability to develop state of the art military technology has improved. Their development of the semi tech might be inevitable but that still doesn’t mean they can catch up within the next 10-15 years. From market perspectives we need to be estimating timelines and probabilities for these possible scenarios. China will likely remain a rational actor until the political situation in China becomes so fraught they have no choice but to make a (currently) sub optimal move like attacking Taiwan. They are playing chess without a queen at the moment imo and would much prefer to get a pawn promoted before they make their next big move. As a rational actor they should remain patient waiting for the perfect chance or until they have no choice.

comment.body: I just want to point out to folks that these huge bear leverage plays could be people shorting the ETF’s not so much for price action but because of the high volatility. Loaning These 3 x ETFs is very expensive but sometimes it makes sense because when the market is chopping about and the decay is so huge a pairs trade with SPY can pay very well even if market goes nowhere much
